text: This is a presentation on endoscopic closure of a diverticular-related colovesicular fistula. Eric Pauly has the following disclosures. The other authors have no disclosures. This is a technique demonstration of endoscopic therapy and definitive closure of a culovesicular fistula following the principles of source control. The procedure highlights the effectiveness of having both the presence of urological expertise in cystoscopy and technical skills in surgical endoscopy available for management of complex defects. This case also focuses on the importance of having alternative treatment modalities for patients who are too high risk for surgery.

Patient is a fifty-nine-year-old male with history of diverticulosis, hypertension, and hepatitis C cirrhosis with a MELD score of sixteen who completed treatment prior to intervention. He presented with complaints of fecal urea and pneumaturia. Colonoscopy showed sigmoid diverticulosis in areas of the granulation tissue but did not localize the fistula. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed a colovesicular fistula between the anterior bladder dome and sigmoid colon.

With confirmed clinical and radiographical diagnosis, patient would undergo a combined procedure in the operating room. The patient was instructed to maintain a clear liquid diet for forty-eight hours prior to the procedure. He also received flea enemolens the evening before and again the morning of the procedure. Given the patient's cirrhosis and poor MELD score, he underwent monitored anesthesia care with propofol to avoid the risk of general anesthesia. Urology performed cystoscopy and cannulated the fistula tract with a wire. Minimally invasive surgery performed a flexible sigmoidoscopy and closed the fistula with an over-the-scope clip. Panendoscopy of the bladder revealed a fistula tract to the left of midline towards the dome of the bladder. Using an angled guide wire advanced through an open-ended catheter, cannulation of the fistula tract was attempted. However, due to difficulty reaching the colon, the scope was switched for a and utilizing a rigid bridge as a guide, a curved catheter was manipulated into the fistula tract and a hydrophilic guide wire was fed through it.

Under fluoroscopy, the catheter is advanced into the colon, and the guide wire is threaded through it. A flexible gastroscope is inserted into the colon, where the wire is identified, grasped, and extracted through the rectum. A GIF-II-T scope, equipped with a XII-VI-T type over-the-scope clip, is then advanced over the wire to the fistula site.

The surgeon uses a combination of wire pull and colon manipulation to position the scope directly facing the defect. Aggressive suction draws the colon wall into the cap, and a clip is fired, appearing well-positioned to close the endoscopic defect. Fluoroscopic evaluation with a bladder catheter confirms closure as contrast does not pass the clip. The fistula on the bladder side is ablated with a holmium laser, and a Foley catheter is placed to drain the bladder.

Patient tolerated the procedure without issues and was discharged home on the same day with a Foley catheter. Medical optimization of the patient's liver disease continued in parallel to our intervention. He was seen in urology clinic post-op day eight where the Foley was removed without issues. The patient reported return of fecal urea and pneumaturia three days after catheter removal, and subsequent CT scan demonstrated recurrence of the fistula. Fortunately, his MELD score had improved from sixteen to nine, allowing him to become a better surgical candidate. Patient underwent a robotic-assisted sigmoidectomy and has been without issues at one month.

This case highlights and teaches the steps necessary to identify the diverticular fistula, manipulate the colon with a wire, and secure a clip on the defect. The ability to have a wire present to visualize and pull and rotate the colon shows how you can change the angle of the colon to maneuver the defect into view. This was all performed while maintaining the principles of source control. Due to the patient's cirrhosis and initial MELD score, an endoscopic intervention was the most appropriate alternative at the time. While we did not achieve long-term success, we were able to help bridge a patient with a high-risk MEL score to surgical therapy. Finally, the assistance from other specialties demonstrates the benefits of a multidisciplinary approach to address complex pathologies.
